coredata.dc:title: Flexible supercapacitor electrode based on lignosulfonate-derived graphene quantum dots/graphene hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lanshu
Cheng, Chen
Yao, Chunli
Jin, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a lignosulfonate has been used for synthesizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with many functional groups by a two-step method. Then a graphene-based composite hydrogel (GH-GQD) was obtained through the modification of the functionalized GQDs. Results showed that the composite hydrogel had a highly porous structure and numerous active sites. Accordingly, the assembled GH-GQD electrode exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 451.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, a good cycle stability (89.0% capacitance retention after 10000 charge/discharge cycles) and mechanical flexibility (93.3% capacitance retention bent to 180°). The present findings indicate a great potential of the graphene hydrogels modified with the functionalized GQDs in fabricating flexible supercapacitor electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignosulfonate
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogel
Flexible supercapacitor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Two-step synthesis method of the GQDs from lignosulfonates.

(a) Raman and (b) XRD spectra of GQDs (Inset is the photographs of GQDs aqueous solution under visible light and UV light of 365 nm); (c) AFM (top) and TEM (below) of GQDs; (d) Lateral size and height distribution of GQDs.
XPS spectra of GQDs: (a) Total XPS spectrum and (b) High-resolution C 1s spectrum.
(a) Schematic diagram of GH-GQD synthesis process; FESEM images of (b) pure GH and (c) GH-GQD.
(a) Total XPS spectra of GH and GH-GQD; High-resolution C 1s spectrum of (b) GH and (c) GH-GQD.
(a) CV curves and (b) GCD curves of GH-GQD electrode; (c) Specific capacitances and (d) EIS of GH and GH-GQD electrodes.
(a) Cycle stability of GH-GQD electrode over 10000 charge/discharge cycles at 5 A g−1 (Inset: the last 7 cycles of GCD); (b) Bending tests under various angles of 0°, 90° and 180°.

head.ce:title: Flexible supercapacitor electrode based on lignosulfonate-derived graphene quantum dots/graphene hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy, Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy
Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Engineering Research Center of Forestry Biomass Materials and Bioenergy, Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a lignosulfonate has been used for synthesizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with many functional groups by a two-step method. Then a graphene-based composite hydrogel (GH-GQD) was obtained through the modification of the functionalized GQDs. Results showed that the composite hydrogel had a highly porous structure and numerous active sites. Accordingly, the assembled GH-GQD electrode exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 451.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, a good cycle stability (89.0% capacitance retention after 10000 charge/discharge cycles) and mechanical flexibility (93.3% capacitance retention bent to 180°). The present findings indicate a great potential of the graphene hydrogels modified with the functionalized GQDs in fabricating flexible supercapacitor electrodes.

Image 104
Schematic illustration of the preparation of GH-GQD.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has been considered as a promising electrode material for flexible supercapacitors [1,2]. Numerous research efforts have been made to prepare three-dimensional (3D) graphene structures, such as hydrogel, aerogel, foam and sponge [3–6]. The 3D porous structures can increase the contact surfaces of electrolyte and provide multiple pathways for efficient ionic transport, thus resulting in good electrochemical performance. However, as the bare 3D graphene framework is inclined to collapse and form irreversible aggregation, nano-sized materials that can render many functional groups and heteroatoms are extremely attractive to micro-regulate 3D porous graphene structures [7]. The efficient functionalization of composite materials can significantly increase pseudo-capacitance through reversible Faradaic redox reactions and further improve the electrochemical properties [7,8]. Thus, it is necessary to develop a nano-sized material with more active sites in a large scale.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are single or few layer graphene nanoparticles and have unique properties of high electron mobility, excellent solubility and easy functionalization [9–12]. Considering these benefits, it is reasonable to expect the functionalized GQDs to modify graphene sheets as excellent electrode materials for flexible and high-performance supercapacitors [13,14]. Recently, the eco-friendly precursors, such as waste biomass [15], food waste [16] and biomass derivatives [17] are considered as potential alternatives because of the advantages of low toxicity, sustainable and renewable. In particular, lignin is the most abundant natural aromatic polymers and commonly used to produce biofuels and high-value added chemicals [18–21]. Lignosulfonate, as one of the representative lignin derivatives, is a by-product from waste liquor of sulfite pulping. Owning to the existence of numerous functional groups (such as sulfonic, carbonyl, methoxyl, phenolic hydroxyl groups) on the surface of lignosulfonates, it is encouraged to convert them into nano-sized product with more active sites and further modify the graphene-based materials.

Thus, in this work, biomass lignosulfonates were employed to prepare GQDs using a two-step method of oxidative cleavage and sp2 hybridization. Then the obtained GQDs were further used to modify graphene hydrogel (named GH-GQD) by a hydrothermal process. Lignosulfonate-derived GQDs with many functional groups can offer enough active sites and graphene sheets with self-assembly ability can provide multileveled porous skeleton for the supercapacitor electrodes. The combination structures enable fast ion/electron transfer between porous electrode materials and electrolytes, and thus result in the desired electrochemical performances. This study broadens high-valued utilization of biomass waste and provides new ideas for the development of renewable and environment-friendly flexible energy storage devices.

First, 3 g of lignosulfonate (LS) (Mw~4000, Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized (DI) water, then adding 5.0 mL of nitric acid drop by drop while stirring continuously. The mixture was treated by ultrasound for 12 h and then diluted with DI water. The brown dispersion was dialyzed in a dialysis bag to remove acid and subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave to carbonize at 180 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to remove undissolved carbon materials. The supernatant was dialyzed overnight, and the purified lignosulfonate-derived GQDs were stored for subsequent use.

The graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite by modified Hummer's methods, and the details were reported before [22]. GH-GQD composite was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method as follows. 15 mL of GO (4.2 mg mL−1), 3.56 mL of GQD (5.9 mg mL−1) and 12.94 mL of DI water were mixed together to form a homogeneous dispersion with a final GO concentration of 2 mg mL−1 (the mass ratio of GO to GQD is 3:1). Then the dispersion was placed in Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the obtained black hydrogel was washed thoroughly with DI water and then sealed with cling film. The pure GH without adding GQD solution was prepared with the same process for comparison.

Using 316L stainless steel fabric (SSF) as flexible substrate, the electrode was prepared by pressing pieces of hydrogel on SSF under 20 MPa for 10 min (the active mass is ~0.5 mg). The fabricated GH and GH-GQD electrodes were immersed in 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous for electrochemical measurements.

The morphology and crystalline structure of lignosulfonate-derived GQD were studied systematically. Raman spectrum was measured on a LabRam HR Raman Spectrometer fitted with a 532 nm laser beam. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained with a Bruker D8 using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were recorded on a JEOL JEM 2100 system. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a Bruker Dimension Icon system. In addition, the morphologies of pure GH and GH-GQDs were examined by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JSM-7800F). XPS analysis was performed with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV).

All the electrochemical measurements of the electrodes were carried out on CHI 660D workstation (Shanghai ChenHua Instruments Co, China) in 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte using a three-electrode system, in which platinum foil electrode (2 cm × 2 cm) and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) acted as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves measured at various current densities of 0.5–20 A g−1 and cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves measured at various scan rates of 5–200 mV s−1 were operated at a potential window from −0.2–0.8 V. The specific capacitances (
C
, F g−1) were calculated from GCD curves according to the equation
C = i
×
Δ t /
(
m
× Δ V
)
, where
i
is the discharging current,
Δ t
is the discharging time,
m
is the active mass of the electrode and
Δ V
is the potential window. Besides, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were detected at a frequency ranging from 10 mHz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The cycle stability of the GH-GQD electrode was evaluated by repeated GCD tests at a current density of 5 A g−1 for 10000 cycles, and the bending tests were performed at different angles of 0°, 90° and 180° at 5 A g−1.

Lignosulfonates are derived from waste liquor of sulfite pulping and formed by the direct combination of sulfite/bisulfite and hydroxyl groups on lignin molecules. And the lignosulfonates contain a large number of oxygen and sulfur elements. As shown in Fig. 1 , the lignosulfonate-derived GQDs were synthesized by a two-step method. Firstly, macromolecule lignosulfonates were soaked in concentrated nitric acid with constant ultrasound and occurred oxidative cleavage into small molecules. This process introduced many nitrite ions. Then, the monolayer or few layers of GQDs could be obtained by sp2 hybridization though a hydrothermal treatment. The stable GQDs had a high concentration of 5.9 mg mL−1 and the yield rate reached up to 29.5%.

Raman spectroscopy is a popular technique for the qualitative analysis of the graphene materials. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the D band at 1385 cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and regarded as the disorder band or the defect band. The G peak is a sharp band that appears around 1580 cm−1, which is an in-plane vibrational mode involving the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [23]. The intensity ratio of the disordered D band and ordered G band (
I
D
/
I
G
) for the GQDs is 0.62, indicating a high degree of graphitization of carbonaceous materials [24]. Moreover, the second-order 2D band is rated to the number of graphene layers [25]. It is observed that a wider 2D peak is centered at 2876 cm−1, which may be attributed to the absence of interlayer interactions between graphene planes [24]. Fig. 2b shows the typical XRD spectrum of GQDs. The GQDs have a broader (002) peak located at 24.2°. The interlayer spacing is 0.367 nm, which is bigger than that of graphite (0.334 nm) [26]. Such result could be attributed to the O, N, S-containing groups existed on the edges or surfaces of GQDs. However, the interlayer spacing of GQDs is smaller than that of the reported graphene oxide (0.386 nm) [27], suggesting that the GQDs may be only functionalized on the edges due to the very small size [28]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2b, the functionalized GQDs aqueous dispersion could exhibit bright green fluorescence under irradiation with a 365 nm UV light. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2c and d, the AFM image demonstrates the topographic morphology of the lignosulfonate-derived GQDs. The heights of GQDs are mostly between 0.3 and 0.7 nm, indicating the GQDs in water has a uniform dispersion and most of them are single layer. The TEM image in Fig. 2c shows a relatively narrow size distribution of 8–10 nm in diameter, which are summarized in Fig. 2d.

XPS tests were carried out to verify the chemical composition of GQDs. As seen in Fig. 3 a, the total XPS spectrum shows a dominant graphitic C1s peak at 284.13 eV (75.39%), O1s peak at 531.73 eV (19.89%), N1s peak at 399.17 eV (3.18%) and S2p peak at 167.53 eV (1.54%), demonstrating that many functional groups are preserved in GQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 3b is deconvoluted into three typical peaks at 284.14, 285.7 and 288.11 eV, corresponding to the functional groups C–C/C=C, C–N and C=O/O–C=O (carbonyl/carboxyl), respectively. Such results indicate that GQDs could be functionalized by hydroxyl, nitrite, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups, confirming the successful synthesis of O, N and S co-doped CQDs.

To explore the advantages of the lignosulfonate-derived GQDs and their potential application in fabricating electrode materials for flexible supercapacitors. The graphene-based composite hydrogel was prepared based on GO sheets modified by the functionalized GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the aqueous dispersion of GQDs and GO sheets were uniformly mixed at a mass ratio of 1:3 and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. During hydrothermal process, the graphene sheets modified with GQDs would self-assemble into a composite hydrogel with 3D network structure.

For comparison, FESEM images of the freeze-dried GH and GH-GQD at the same magnification were shown in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. Both hydrogels have interconnected channels and porous network structures. Such structures like sponges are conducive to electrolyte penetration, and thus are suitable electrode materials for supercapacitors [29]. However, partial overlaps and agglomerations exist in pure GH (yellow circles in Fig. 4b). And GH-GQD composites have more micro-/meso-pores and obvious thinner pore walls (red circles in Fig. 4c), which are due to the interactions between the functionalized GQDs and graphene sheets. The GQDs with abundant functional groups could interact with graphene sheets though covalent/noncovalent bonds, such as ether linkage, hydrogen bonds and π-π interactions. Thus, the graphene sheets were uniformly dispersed and assembled into complex 3D interconnected porous structures.

The GH-GQD composites together with the pure GH were further studied by XPS analysis. Compared to the XPS spectrum of pure GH, the presence of heteroatoms N (2.31%), S (0.82%) and the increased peak intensity of O atoms are caused by nitric acid treatment process and sulfonic acid groups on CQDs, which also indicate that GQDs are successfully incorporated into GH-GQD composites (Fig. 5 a). The high-resolution C1s spectra for GH and GH-GQD are shown in Fig. 5b and c, respectively. The percentage of C–C/C=C bond in GH-GQD composites (55.3%) is slightly decreased in comparison with that in pure GH (60.4%). Especially, the C–N bonds account for a relatively large proportion (26.9%) in GH-GQD composites. Such results indicate that a great many of N atoms are introduced in GQDs by nitric acid treatment and still retained in GH-GQD after hydrothermal process. However, there is no C–O bond in GH-GQD, which may be due to the O atoms in pure GH are substituted by N atoms or part of the C–OH bonds are converted to C=O during thermal reduction treatment. It can also be seen that the percentages of C=O/O–C=O bond in both GH and GH-GQD samples are impressive. These linkage bonds can participate in Faradaic reactions to provide improved pseudo capacitance for supercapacitors [30].

The electrochemical properties of GH and GH-GQD electrodes were investigated in 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte using a three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 6 a, all CV curves of GH-GQD electrode with a potential window of −0.2-0.8 V exhibit a quasi-rectangular shape even at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1. This indicates the electrode materials with a good rate capability have a dominant double-layer capacitance characteristic [31,32]. However, some slight deformation with weakly broadened humps in CV curves is due to the pseudo-capacitance. This could be contributed by the reversible redox reactions of the O-containing functional groups (C=O/O–C=O) at the surface of materials [33,34]. Moreover, the introduced O, N and S heteroatoms can induce numerous redox active sites by modifying the acid-basic feature and electron donor-acceptor characteristic of carbon materials, further providing extra pseudo-capacitance and improving the specific capacitance [35].

GCD curves for GH-GQD electrode at different current densities of 0.5–20 A g−1 were shown in Fig. 6b. All GCD curves with almost straight lines and symmetric triangles demonstrate that the composite electrode has a good electrochemical reversibility and an ideal double-layer capacitance behavior [36]. Additionally, the curves at various current densities exhibit a very small IR drop, indicating a good discharging efficiency. The specific capacitances of the GH and GH-GQD electrodes were calculated according to GCD curves and plotted in Fig. 6c. The GH-GQD electrode reaches a maximum specific capacitance of 451.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, which is almost double higher than that of GH (230.6 F g−1). When increasing the current density, the specific capacitance of both electrodes gradually decreased. This could be due to the fast charge/discharge processes directly result in the reduction of the adsorption ability for the porous structure. At the high current density of 20 A g−1, the obtained specific capacitance is still 274 F g−1 (60.7% retention) for GH-GQD electrode.

EIS was used to characterize the ionic and charge transfer resistances of the GH and GH-GQD electrodes (Fig. 6d). At low-frequency region, the GH-GQD electrode shows a more vertical line than GH electrode, indicating an enhanced capacitive behavior [37]. The magnified high-frequency region in the inset of Fig. 6d shows the semicircles of the GH and GH-GQD electrodes. Both them show a small intercept (~0.64 Ω) at the real axis, indicating an efficient ionic transport rate. Moreover, the charge transfer resistance corresponding to the semicircle for GH-GQD electrode (~1.33 Ω) is much smaller than that of GH electrode (~5.28 Ω). This result indicates that the functionalized GQDs could reduce the ion-transport distance and enable easy access of electrolyte into the inner pores of composite hydrogel.

The cycle stability and mechanical flexibility are important for practical application of supercapacitor electrode. Fig. 7 a shows the charge/discharge cycles of GH-GQD electrode at a current density of 5 A g−1. The specific capacitance retention is maintained at 89.0% of its original value after 10000 cycles, indicating a good cyclability and durability. It also can be observed that the GCD curves exhibit almost symmetrical triangular shapes even at the last 7 cycles (Inset in Fig. 7a), which confirm a high degree of reversibility for charge storage. Besides, the GH-GQD electrode was bent to 90° and 180°, respectively. Results show the GH-GQD electrode has excellent flexibility with 98.5% capacitance retention at a bending angle of 90° and 93.3% capacitance retention at a bending angle of 180° (Fig. 7b). The GH-GQD electrode has not only high specific capacitance but also good durability, showing that the functionalized GQDs from lignosulfonate are promising in the modification of graphene hydrogel for supercapacitor electrodes. The superior electrochemical properties of GH-GQD electrode can be explained as follows: 1) the numerous heteroatoms and functional groups can provide effective redox active sites to enhance the specific capacitance; 2) the abundant pores can fasten the ions diffusion and provide unblocked channels to improve the adsorption ability [38]. These fully demonstrate the advantages of the GH-GQD electrode materials in energy storage.

In summary, lignosulfonate-derived GQDs with numerous heteroatoms and functional groups were prepared and successfully used to modify graphene hydrogel for supercapacitor electrodes. The functionalized GQDs have high quantum yield, bright fluorescence and uniform graphitized structure, which provide effective redox active sites for the GH-GQD composites. The assembled GH-GQD electrode with highly porous network structure exhibit high specific capacitance (451.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1), good cycle stability (89.0% capacitance retention after 10000 cycles) and excellent mechanical flexibility (93.3% capacitance retention bent to 180°) in 1.0 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. This study achieves the high-valued utilization of low-valued biomass wastes and opens up a new way for the preparation of flexible supercapacitor electrodes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work greatly thanked to the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31470605).
